title: Sowell’s Law: How Low Margins Create High Returns
body_html:
The essay leans on Thomas Sowell’s supermarket example because it destroys a popular intuition: that lower prices necessarily mean weaker business economics. In reality, a business can earn thin margins on each sale and still generate excellent returns if capital turns over quickly enough. That is the difference between accounting optics and economic substance.

Supermarkets are a perfect case because they make the math intuitive. A penny of profit on the dollar sounds unimpressive until the same inventory cycles through the store again and again. What matters is not the headline margin but the velocity with which committed capital comes back and can be reused.

This logic generalizes far beyond groceries. Many great businesses win by compressing unit margins while improving scale, turnover, and downstream leverage. Lower prices attract more customers, deepen habits, and often widen the moat. The gain is not extracted from the consumer; it is created by reorganizing production better than rivals do.

That is why deflation is not anti-business. In competitive markets, lower prices can be the very mechanism by which superior businesses demonstrate superiority.
